coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) is one of the very important zero dimensional nanostructure which are known as the semiconductor nanocrystals. These nanostructures are of great interest due to their unique optical properties such as high absorption amplitude, strong fluorescence and high resistance to photobleaching. Therefore, quantum dots are widely used as a fluorescence indicator in vitro and in vivo in the field of bioimaging. In addition, quantum dots are widely used in the preparation of multifunctional thranostic nanomaterials. Although organic dye and quantum dots synthesized with heavy metals has been used for cell imaging in recent years, but the production of quantum dots from two-dimensional nanostructures due to having unique properties such as more stable fluorescence, less toxicity and more eco-friendly has recently received more attention. Graphene oxide, molybdenum disulfide, and black phosphorus are the most popular two-dimensional layered nanostructures used to synthesize quantum dots. These quantum dots are used to imaging different types of cancers. Recently, they have also been used in techniques such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) for normal cell imaging. In this review the synthesis methods as well as surface modifications of QDs derived from two dimensional nanostructures are discussed. Furthermore the newest applications of these QDs in the field of cancer cellular imaging are reviewed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of recent advances in GQD synthesis via top-down and bottom-up methods.

Application of GQDs in cancer cells imaging. A) Fluorescent images of HeLa cells after incubation with A) GQDs‐F for 4 h and B) GQDs‐P for 4 h. Stability comparison of the fluorescence signals of HeLa cells labeled with GQDs‐F (C), GQDs‐P (D), and FITC (E). B) Confocal laser scanning microscopy of Hela human cervical carcinoma cells, A549 adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells, and HEK293A normal human embryonic kidney cells incubated with GQD–FA. C) CLSM images of U251 Glioma cells treated with DOX-GQDs-RGD after incubation for 2, 8 and 16 h excited by a 405-nm laser. Reproduced from [55,77,83] with permission from the Elsevier.
Schematic illustration of recent advances in MoS2 QDs synthesis methods.

Application of MoS2QDs in cancer cells imaging. A) In vitro fluorescence imaging of SW480 tumor cells with MoS2QDs. In vivo fluorescence images of mice bearing renal carcinoma tumors pre- and post-injection (1 h and 24 h) of MoS2QDs. The red circle in pictures indicated tumor region. B) Down-conversion and up-conversion cellular imaging of MoS2QDs under 460 nm and 800 nm excitation respectively. C) T1-weighted in vivo MR imaging of mice pre- and post-injection of Mn-MoS2QDs (left) and AS1411-Mn-MoS2QDs nanoprobe (right) at different time points, the white arrows indicate tumor. Reproduced from [99,105,106] with permission from the Elsevier. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of recent advances in BP QDs synthesis methods.

Application of BPQDs in cancer cells imaging. A) Time-dependent photo-acoustic images of the MCF-7 cells in the xenografted tumor in the BALB/c nude mice after intravenous injection of TiL4@BPQDs. B) fluorescence images of Hep G2 and 4 T1 cells incubated with RdB/PEG-BPQDs. C) In vitro imaging of 293 T cells treated with FA-PEG@BPQD@DOX and PEG@BPQD@DOX. In vivo thermal images of mice with 293 T tumors irradiated at 808 nm. The tumor photo-acoustic images after injecting PEG@BPQD@DOX and FA-PEG@BPQD@DOX NPs with irradiation at 680 nm. Reproduced from [122,124,125].

Application of GCNQDs in cancer cell imaging. A) Confocal fluorescence image microphotograph of the Hela cells incubated with g-C3N4 QDs for 5 h. B) Optical and NIR images of a GCNQDs treated mouse. Reproduced from [136,137].

Application of MXene QDs in cancer cell imaging. A) PA image of the MXene QDs for different concentrations at 680 nm. B) PA image of tumors in mice at 680 nm before and after administration of MXene QDs. Reproduced from [140].

Application of GeQDs in cancer cell imaging. A) In vitro photoacoustic imaging of PEG@GeQDs solutions (Red: high, Black: low). B) PA images of the tumors at different time points. C) Photothermal images of mice (G1: Control; G2: NIR; G3: PEG@GeQDs; G4: PEG@GeQDs + NIR; (808 nm). Reproduced from [145]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Application of AMQDs in cancer cell imaging. A) Fluorescence image of MCF-7 cells treated with PEG-coated AMQDs. B) Photohermal images of MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice (G1: Saline; G2: only NIR; G3: only PEG-coated AMQDs; G4: PEG-coated AMQDs + NIR; 808 nm). Reproduced from [147].
Schematic of the present review, including the synthesis methods, functionalized modification and application in cancer cell imaging of novel 2D nanostructures based QDs.
The application of graphene quantum dots in cancer cell imaging.

Synthesis methods/ Modification/ cell line
properties/applications
Peptid nanofiber-Graphene quantum dots
electrochemistry method/ Protein nanofiber (PNF)/ Hela cell
PNF-GQD have strong fluorescence and good dispersity and biocompatibility and can efficiently label live HeLa cells [76].
Fluorinated Graphene QuantumDots
microwave-assisted technique combined withhydrothermal treatment/ Fluorine/ Hela cell
Fluorinated GQDs was particularly suitable for long-term and real-time cellular imaging due to ultrahigh pH and photostability (Fig. 2A)[77].
Graphene oxide quantum dots
pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) technique/ ---- / human pancreatic cancer cell PanC-1
GOQDs exhibit excellent optoelectronic properties which can be successfully utilized in cancer cell imaging [78].
graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots
hydrothermal-carbonization process/ ----/ breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231)
Confocal laser scanning microscopy images showed that the GO-PEI-GQDs attach the surface of the breast cancer cells with strong interaction[79].
graphene quantum dots
hydrothermal method/ -----/ human cervical carcinoma cells (Ca ski)
Cancer cells staining with GQDs exhibit clearer morphology than the cells in the bright field state[80].
folic acidmodified graphene oxide quantum dots
Acid exfoliation method/ folic acid/ HaCaT cells
FA modified GOQDs were successfully used in order to fluorescence lifetime imaging of HaCaT cells[81].
Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots Decorated Magnetic Nanoplatform
Hydrothermal method/ ----/ Hep G2 liver cancer tumor
GOQDs decorated Fe3O4 nanoparticles based on two-photon luminescence platform can be efficient for imaging of Hep G2 hepatocellular carcinoma tumor cell[82].
Folic acid conjugated graphene quantum dots
chemical oxidation/ folic acid/ HeLa cells
FA conjugated GQDs with Inherent fluorescence and without employing external dyes, facilitated simultaneous localization of the drug carrier and the loaded drug (Fig. 2B)[55].
arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) conjugated graphene quantum dots
thermally exfoliated method/ RGD/ U251 human glioma cells
RGD conjugated GQDs display no toxicity and penetrated into cancer cell nuclei after 16 h of incubation and emitted green fluorescence (Fig. 2C)[83].
graphene quantum dots
Solvothermal method/ -----/ A-375 (human malignantmelanoma cells) and HeLa (human cervical carcinoma)
Fluorescence microscopy image showed that GQDs successfully internalized in the A-375 and HeLa cells and are mainly localized in the cytoplasm region[84].
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal method/ polyethyleneimine (PEI) or (3-carboxyl) phenyl bromide phosphine (TPP)/ Hela cell.
Result showed that the imaging properties of GQDs were comparable to the commercial fluorescent dye, such as DAPI and MitoTracker® Red[85].

Synthesis methods/ modifications/ cell line
properties/applications
Black phosphorus quantum dots
Sonication exfoliation technique/ titanium sulfonate ligand/ MCF-7 breast cancer (in vitro) - MCF-7 Tumors in Mice (in vivo).

Quantum dots modified with titanium sulfonate are more efficient than gold nanoribons in photoacoustic imaging and have a high efficiency in the diagnosis of cancer cells both in the in vitro in vivo (Fig. 6A) [122].
Polydopamine-functionalized black phosphorus quantum dots
Sonication exfoliation technique/ polydopamine/ A375 cell line (in vitro) - nude mice bearing A375 human melanoma tumor (in vivo).

Black phosphorus quantum dots modified with polydopamine has stronger and more durable PAI contrast property; excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability; and improved photothermal performance on cancer cells / tumors [123].
PEGylated black phosphorus quantumDots
sonication exfoliation technique/ Polyethylene glycol/ Human hepatocellular carcinoma (Hep G2) cells-4 T1 murine breast cancer cells.

A clear fluorescence signal (RdB) was found in Hep G2 and 4 T1 cells and without BPQDs, no fluorescence signal was found in cancer cells, which indicated the potential of the PEG-BPQDs as a bioimaging platform(Fig. 6B) [124].
PEGylated-folic acid–modified black phosphorus quantumDots
sonication exfoliation technique/ Polyethylene glycol and folic acid/ Human embryonic kidney 293 T (HEK 293 T) cells
FA PEG@BPQD@DOX is a good candidate for thermal and photo acoustic imaging of Human embryonic kidney 293 T cells (Fig. 6C) [125].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) is one of the very important zero dimensional nanostructure which are known as the semiconductor nanocrystals. These nanostructures are of great interest due to their unique optical properties such as high absorption amplitude, strong fluorescence and high resistance to photobleaching. Therefore, quantum dots are widely used as a fluorescence indicator in vitro and in vivo in the field of bioimaging. In addition, quantum dots are widely used in the preparation of multifunctional thranostic nanomaterials. Although organic dye and quantum dots synthesized with heavy metals has been used for cell imaging in recent years, but the production of quantum dots from two-dimensional nanostructures due to having unique properties such as more stable fluorescence, less toxicity and more eco-friendly has recently received more attention. Graphene oxide, molybdenum disulfide, and black phosphorus are the most popular two-dimensional layered nanostructures used to synthesize quantum dots. These quantum dots are used to imaging different types of cancers. Recently, they have also been used in techniques such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) for normal cell imaging. In this review the synthesis methods as well as surface modifications of QDs derived from two dimensional nanostructures are discussed. Furthermore the newest applications of these QDs in the field of cancer cellular imaging are reviewed.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, various methods have been proposed for cell imaging, including optical and fluorescent microscopy [1–3]. Optical imaging is a useful tool for detecting neoplastic changes in vivo and does not require a biopsy. Images in optical microscopes are obtained through magnified lenses and visible to near infrared light. Fluorescence microscope is a contrast enhancing optical microscope in which the source of light is UV. Examination of samples in these microscopes is performed by labeling parts of the sample with fluorescent dyes. Fluorescent materials absorb light at the UV wavelength and radiate at a longer wavelength in the visible spectrum. Finally, the resulting image is created by the light reflected from the sample. Microscopic imaging techniques have advantages such as cheapness, non-invasive, fast and ease of use, but they also have disadvantages [4–6]. Although, many efforts have been made to improve the sensitivity, accuracy and efficiency of fluorescent microscopy, there are still some limitations to this approach [7,8]. At first, the cell may emit fluorescence through its proteins and cause disturbance to the labeled molecules. The next problem in this method is that the fluorescence emission time is not long, so it is necessary to use probe compounds that emit near-infrared light and have higher optical stability [9]. The use of organic dye molecules seems to entrepreneur in this regard, but their cytotoxicity and photobleaching limit their application [10,11]. With development in nanotechnology science, the use of nanostructures with fluorescence properties such as quantum dots has been increased [12–16]. Quantum Dots (QDs), often described as ‘artificial atoms,’ are semiconductor nanocrystals that are in the nanoscale region in all dimensions (so called zero-dimensional nanostructures) and have a size less than 10 nm [11]. These nanostructures have unique properties, such as high stability, large surface area and good biocompatibility [17]. There are many methods for synthesizing QDs, but they are generally divided into top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top down method include molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), ion implantation, electron -beam lithography, and X-ray lithography and bottom-up method include wet-chemical and vapor-phase methods [17–21]. Depending on the synthesis conditions (temperature and duration) and the materials used, the size and shape of the quantum dots can be precisely controlled [22,23]. The range of applications of quantum dots is very wide and includes areas such as optoelectronics, sensors, industry, supercapacitor, environment, drug delivery and bioamaging [24–32]. In recent years, the use of two-dimensional nanostructures such as graphene, molybdenum disulfide, black phosphorus, etc. in the QD form has increased in drug delivery and cancer cellular imaging [33–36]. It seems that QDs derived from 2D nanostructures are more efficient and useful than conventional quantum dots. For example, semiconductor quantum dots such as PbS, CdHgTe and CdSe usually contain heavy metals that lead to their high toxicity [37–40]. In addition, with other properties such as low solubility in water and flashing, their application in imaging is more limited. Also, with having a higher molecular weight than biological molecules, it affects the function of target molecules. Therefore, exploring alternative fluorophore molecules in bioimaging applications seems necessary [41–44]. Previous studies in the in vitro and in vivo showed that 2D nanostructures based QDs at low concentrations have little cytotoxicity. In cell imaging, the size and concentration of quantum dots are two key factors that affect cytotoxicity [45]. For example, Jiang et al reported that graphene quantum dots with a small size and concentration less than 50 μg mL−1 have a cytotoxicity of less than 10%, while at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1 they have a cytotoxicity of more than 20%, which indicating that the low-concentrated GQDs have well biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [46]. It is also reported that BPQDs at a concentration up to 200 mg mL−1 had significant apoptotic effects on HeLa cells [47]. Therefore, finding the optimal concentration that does not have cellular toxicity is an important issue in the application of these nanostructures in the field of medicine [48,49]. Because of the importance of two dimensional nanostructures in the synthesis and developing the nano-probe for imaging, in this study we focused on synthesis methods as well as surface modifications of QDs prepared from two dimensional layered materials. Furthermore the newest applications of these 2D nanostructures based QDs in the field of cellular imaging are reviewed (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots in cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanographene oxides have been proposed as a carrier for drug delivery [50,51]. Because of their low fluorescence efficiency, this group of nanostructures has a limitation to cellular uptake detection in living organisms as well as in cells [52]. Using a fluorescent dye or a quantum dot next to them may be helpful, but the toxicity of these elements is another limiting factor [53,54]. For this reason, it is necessary to develop nanosystems that are used in both imaging and drug delivery without need to the fluorescent dye [55]. Graphene Quantum Dot (GQDs) is one of the most attractive and newest members of the graphene family, which has many applications in the field of biomedical, including imaging [56]. These nanomaterials are very popular because of their photoluminescence emission. GQDs have many advantages such as low toxicity and eco-friendly, over other quantum dots (such as cadmium selenide and cadmium telluride, which have heavy metals) [57,58].

Several methods including acidic exfoliation and oxidation, hydrothermal reaction, solvothermal reaction, electrochemical oxidation, and among others which are belonging to top-down methods; have been reported for the synthesis of GQDs in the various size as well as photoluminescences (Fig. 1 ).

In the acidic exfoliation method, carbon compounds such as graphene and graphene oxide are refluxed with 6 M nitric acid for 6 h. After cooling at room temperature, the suspension is centrifuged and the supernatant is separated and heated to 200 °C to evaporate the solvent. Finally a brown precipitate as GQDs is obtained. In the hydrothermal method, pure oxidized graphene plates are dispersed in deionized water and heated in a Teflon autoclave for 10 h at 200 °C. After cooling at room temperature, the suspension is filtered. In order to remove large nanoparticles with weak fluorescence, the solution is dialyzed for 24 h and finally GQDs with small size and strong fluorescence are obtained. In the solvothermal method, quantum dots are obtained from the heat of graphene oxide in the presence of DMF as a solvent at a temperature of 200 °C for 4.5 h. In the electrochemical method, GQDs are obtained by applying a CV scan within the potential region of ± 3.0 V at a scan rate of 0.5 V/s to the graphene film in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution [34,59–66].

Catalyzed cage-opening, precursor pyrolysis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and among others that belonging to bottom-up methods have been used for fabricated GQDs through a series of chemical reactions starting from small molecules (Fig. 1). In the Catalyzed cage-opening method, with induce high-temperature the fullerene molecules embedded in the surface of ruthenium crystals are fragmented to produce carbon clusters. Eventually, with diffusion and aggregation of these clusters, GQDs are formed. In the precursor pyrolysis method, citric acid is heated to 200 °C. After about 5 min, citric acid liquefies and after 30 min, the color of the liquid turns orange, which indicates the formation of GQDs. In the microwave assisted method, a significant amount of GQDs is obtained by microwave radiation to graphite in the presence of H2SO4 as well as KMnO4 for one hour [67–71].

In hydrothermal, solvothermal and acid exfoliation methods, synthesis from bulk material allows large scale production of GQDs. In addition, GQDs from these methods have a large number of functional groups on their layers, which have high water solubility and facilitate their modification. Therefore, they will be highly effective for targeted imaging. However, these methods require advanced equipment such as autoclaves, acidic and organic solvents, and harsh conditions that lead to environmental damage. In addition, it has no control over the size and morphology of GQDs. The electrochemical method with selective oxidation of the precursor through the applied electric potential provides a more accurate method for the synthesis of GQDs than the methods mentioned above. In contrast, GQDs synthesized by the bottom-up method have a regular shape and size and have stable properties. However, due to the nature of bottom-up methods, a low number of functional groups are created on the edges of QDs. Therefore, they have less water solubility and their modification in order to be used for targeted imaging will probably be multi-step [45,72]. After synthesis, surface modification of graphene quantum dots has been considered by many researchers in order to increase biocompatibility, drug delivery, targeting efficiency, imaging, and toxicity reduction [73–75]. However, GQDs, with significant properties that mentioned above are good candidates for cancer cell detection in both in vitro and in vivo conditions. Table 1 summarizes the applications of graphene quantum dots in cancer cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molybdenum di sulfide quantum dots in cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Following the discovery of graphene as the most popular two-dimensional nanostructure, researchers' efforts to find other two-dimensional nanostructures have become increasingly [86,87]. Meanwhile, transition metal dichalcogenids, which belong to a large family of layered compounds, have attracted the attention of researchers [88,89]. The chemical structure of these nanostructures is X-M-X, where M = Mo, W; X = S, Se, Te; and the bond between the atoms is strong covalent and between the layers is weak van der walls [90–92].

Among transition metal dichalcogenids, molybdenum disulfide nanosheets (MoS2 nanosheets) are more commonly used [93,94]. MoS2 nanosheets has many applications in biomedical, including photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, biosensing, thranostic, gene therapy, and also bioimaging [95–97]. Although there is a lot of research on the preparation as well as the application of MoS2 nanosheets in the field of medicine, but the use of molybdenum disulfide quantum dot (MoS2 QDs) in biomaging has been reported very little [35].

There are many methods for the preparation of MoS2QDs, including chemical intercalation, electrochemical exfoliation, liquid solvent exfoliation, electron fenton reaction, and hydrothermal reaction which are mentioned in the text briefly (Fig. 3
).

In the chemical intercalation method, using lithium (Li) ion and during three stages of exfoliation, MoS2QDs are synthesis from MoS2 nanosheets. Li+ ions are placed between the MoS2 plates and prevent them from re-adhering. Finally, using sonication, the MoS2 nanosheets are broken down into quantum dots. In the electrochemical exfoliation method, by applying the appropriate voltage and in the presence of ionic solutions such as lithium bis-trifluoromethyl sulfonylimide (LiTFSI), MoS2QDs with very small size are obtained. In the liquid solvent exfoliation method, the basis synthesis of MoS2QDs is sonication. This method uses different solvents such as water/ethanol and NMP. After the MoS2 powder is dispersed in the solvent, the bath or probe sonicated is performed for a specified period of time, and finally, MoS2QDs are separated by centrifugation. In the electron fenton reaction method, hydroxyl ions are used to etching MoS2 nanosheets. In this method, the nanosheets are uniformly rupture and turned into quantum dot-like pieces. Production of QDs in this method is directly related to the increase of hydroxyl ions. In the hydrothermal method, MoS2 powder or its derivatives are subjected to high temperature and pressure for periods of more than 15 h, and finally MoS2 quantum dots are synthesized [98–103]. Table 2 summarizes the applications of MoS2QDs in the bioimaging of cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Black phosphorus quantum dots in cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Black phosphorus is another two-dimensional nanostructure that has received a lot of attention due to its unique electronic and optical properties [107,108]. This two-dimensional nanostructure, like graphene, is composed of only one element (phosphorus) and is widely used in bioimaging and drug delivery due to its high biocompatibility, biological activity, biodegradability [109,110]. In addition, because phosphorus is an essential element in the body, the decomposed product of this nanostructure (PO4-3) is very low risk, and that's why it's a good candidate for drug delivery [111,112].

Black phosphorus nanosheets have also been widely used in areas such as photo acoustic imaging, photothermal therapy and photodynamic therapy [113]. Black Phosphorus Quantum Dots (BPQDs) are zero-dimensional nanostructures derived from black phosphorus nanosheets that were first introduced in 2015 [47].

There are many ways for synthesis of black phosphorus quantum dots, including ultrasonic exfoliation, electrochemical exfoliation, solvothermal, milling crash, blender breaking and plused laser irradiation, which classified into two general categories: top-down and bottom-up methods (Fig. 5
). Ultrasonic exfoliation method is one of the most popular methods for the synthesis of BPQDs. In this method, BP powder was mixed with N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent, which is the most commonly used solvent, and milled in a mortar. Then mixture was sonicated and centrifuged to remove the supernatant, which contains BPQDs.

In the electrochemical exfoliation method, a potential difference was induced to the BP nanoparticles from two different directions, which causes the nanoparticles to fragment and create BPQDs. In the solvothermal method, the BP powder dispersed in NMP was placed at 140 °C for more than 10 h under intense stirring and nitrogen to form BPQDs. In the milling crush method, black phosphorus crystals are milled in mortar and then ultrasonic in the solvent. Then BP suspensions were milled in a solvent with ultratorax in DMF at high speed, and finally the quantum dots are synthesized. In the blender breaking method, the bulk BP is dispersed in DMSO and then mixed with the kitchen house blender. Due to the turbulent flow rate, the layers of BP are destroyed and the formation of QDs is observed by changing the color to brown. In the plused laser irradiation method, the plused laser is irradiated on black phosphorus crystals dispersed in diethyl ether, and after about 20 min, quantum dots are formed [114–119].

These QDs are used in the fields of bioimaging, cancer therapy, fluorescence sensing, photovoltaic devices, and optoelectronics [47]. Usually, Surface modification of black phosphorus quantum dots are done, because they are not highly stable in the aqueous medium and may react with oxygen and oxidized. Polymer modified black phosphorus quantum dot, assembly off quantum dot in device, quantum dot molecule complex, quantum dot doping film and quantum dot nanosheet hybrid are among the common modifications that can be performed on black phosphorus quantum dot [117,120,121].

The application of black phosphorus quantum dots in the field of cancer cell imaging is summarized in three categories: fluorescence imaging, photoacoustic imaging, and thermal imaging. Table 3 summarizes the applications of black phosphorus quantum dots in bioimaging of cancer cells.

In addition to the three mentioned 2D nanostructure based QDs which discussed above in details, there are also other QDs using in the cancer cell imaging. Graphene carbon nitride quantum dots (GCNQDs) as one of the subclass of carbon-based quantum dots has attracted a lot of attention in recent years. These quantum dots have prominent features such as wide emission range, high quantum yield, small size distribution and rapid metabolism [126,127]. Chemical exfoliation, ultrasonication, chemical tailoring, thermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, hydrothermal method, solvothermal method and microwave method are synthesis methods of this group of QDs [128–135]. Zhan and co-worker reported that GCNQDs after 48 h and in the concentration of 100 μg mL−1 have slight toxicity on Hela cells and they proposed that these QDs could be promising candidate for cancer cell imaging (Fig. 7
A) [136]. Also, Liu et al. showed that GCNQDs could produce bright NIR fluorescence in tumor tissue (Fig. 7B) [137].

Another two-dimensional nanostructure that has been introduced in recent years is MXenes, which are defined as ultrathin layers of carbides, nitrides, and carbonitrides [138]. MXenes derived QDs, like other QDs have a fascinating properties, such as natural hydrophilicity, good biocompatibility, ready functionalization, and excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties [139]. In addition to the applications in other fields such as energy, industry, etc., they are also used in cancer cell imaging. Yu and co-worker reported that MXene QDs as highly efficient contrast agents can be a promising candidate for PA imaging (Fig. 8 ). They announced that with increasing of MXene QDs’ concentrations, the PA signal of MXene QDs greatly enhanced [140].

After graphene and black phosphorus, germanene as a new member of the two-dimensional (2D) monoelemental materials has recently attracted much attention in the different areas such as drug delivery, cancer imaging, and etc [141,142]. This nanostructure has a high photothermal conversion efficacy and good biocompatibility [143]. Mechanical cleavage, physical vapor deposition methods and liquid exfoliation are among common methods for synthesis of germanene nanostructures [144]. Germanene quantum dots (GeQDs) are used in photoacoustics and photothermal imaging of cancer cells. Ouyang and colleagues used GeQDs for breast cancer tumors imaging. They stated that the PA signal was directly related to the increase in concentration and time (Fig. 9 A and B). In addition, they reported that GeQDs could be involved in photothermal imaging of cancer cells due to their strong adsorption in the NIR region(Fig. 9C) [145].

Antimonene (AM) is another 2D nanostructure that recently received increasing interest due to its superior physicochemical properties compared with typical 2D NMs (e.g., graphene, MoS2, WS2, and BP). AM possesses great potential for development as a photothermal agent [146]. Therefore, AMQDs is used for photothermal imaging of cancer cells. Tao and co-worker reported that PEG-coated AMQDs can be used to cancer cell imaging both in vitro and in vivo conditions without significant cytotoxicity (Fig. 10 ) [147].

GQDs, MoS2QDs and BPQDs, are highly efficient in cancer cell imaging due to their unique optical properties. These types of quantum dots are less toxic and more biocompatible than conventional quantum dots. The size and concentration of quantum dot used for imaging proses is one of the important factors that affecting the cytotoxicity of these nanostructures. The photoluminescence properties of these nanostructures are very strong and may be influenced by many factors and reduced. The size, surface modification, chemical doping, pH, and solvent medium are the example of these factors. There are many synthesis methods for these nanostructures, most of which placed into the top-down category. The hydrothermal method and the ultrasonic liquid exfoliation method are the most common synthesis methods for GQDs, MoS2QDs and BPQDs, respectively. Each of the synthesis methods mentioned in the text can have advantages and disadvantages, but for synthesis of quantum dots with high size uniformity, it seems necessary to combine two or more synthetic methods. Furthermore, surface modification of quantum dot using different compounds improves their stability and biocompatibility. However, the surface modifications of these nanostructures are limited and it seems that with more studies in this field, more interesting and significant results can be obtained in the field of cancer cell imaging. Although these nanostructures are used in cell imaging, the study in this field is still in its infancy and many important issues and problems need to be addressed. At first, so far most of the studies have been done in the in vitro, and targeted quantum dot should be created, and must be examined more in the in vivo, in order to determine their true effectiveness. In order to identify a specific cancer cell, quantum dot-modified with a specific functional group are required, which increases the accumulation of quantum dot within the tumor tissue. Second, the cytotoxicity of quantum dots with different functional groups should be examined more deeply. Third, fluorescence emissions in high-wavelength regions should be given more attention, as autofluorescence background as well as signal-to-noise ratios are reduced. Fluorescence emission at wavelengths in near-infrared regions is more effective in cancer cell imaging. Finally, it should be noted that the use of GQDs, MoS2QDs and BPQDs in imaging techniques such as CT scan and MRI seems very likely in the near future.
